cataloging_subject.label: The Book of Faith
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Reginald Pecock's single Middle English prose work surviving incompletely in Trinity College Cambridge MS B.14.45: a prologue, ten chapters of Part I, and five chapters of Part II. This subject excludes J. L. Morison's 1909 introduction, summary, punctuation and notes as editorial matter; Henry Wharton's partial 1688 publication; the University of Michigan OCR and page images; the physical codex and its modern photographs; and the checked-in repository XML as distinct transmission layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Book of Faith
catalog_summary.author: Reginald Pecock
catalog_summary.date: Composed c. 1456–1457; attested in the sole known fifteenth-century manuscript; partially printed 1688 and first completely printed from the surviving text in 1909
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of composition unresolved

notes: This is the established modern title used by the current holding institution and scholarship; the manuscript item itself has no heading.

titles.value: Reginald Pecock's Book of Faith: A Fifteenth Century Theological Tractate

assertion.notes: Wharton says the author entitled the whole work A Treatise of Faith, but the current Trinity catalog says that the surviving manuscript item has no heading.

notes: Retained as an attributed authorial title, not as a rubric directly visible at the present opening of B.14.45.

titles.value: Facti sunt filii mei perditi, quia invaluit inimicus: that is to seie in Englisch thus: My sones ben maad lost for the enemye hath had the maistrie
notes: The incipit combines a Latin quotation from Lamentations with Pecock's Middle English rendering.

agents.name: Reginald Pecock
notes: Dates are commonly given as c. 1395–c. 1460; the present manifest does not treat those approximate life dates as work dates.

agents.name: J. L. Morison
notes: Editor and transcriber of the complete surviving text in the immediate 1909 source edition. The consulted title page and institutional catalogs give initials rather than a verified expanded name.

agents.name: Henry Wharton
notes: Editor and transcriber of the partial 1688 publication.

scope.description: The 1909 scholarly edition that immediately underlies the U-M item
The complete surviving medieval text on Morison's printed pp. 109–305

assertion.notes: Morison's preface makes a complete text his first objective and explicitly states his treatment of manuscript spelling, punctuation, symbols, and textual explanations.

scope.description: Isolated apparatus comparisons with Wharton's 1688 partial transcript, not a continuous base text

assertion.notes: At printed p. 236 Morison expressly reports a Wharton reading; the present evidence does not support a claim of systematic full-text collation.

scope.description: Wharton's 1688 edition of selected Part I excerpts and the complete surviving Part II available to him
Selected extracts transcribed directly from the Trinity manuscript

assertion.notes: Kelly dates the work c. 1456–57; Morison argues for about 1456. The range is approximate, not a dated colophon.

date_statements.display: Sole known witness: after the work's c. 1456–1457 composition and within the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The current institutional catalog dates the codex only to the fifteenth century; the lower bound is inferred from the independently supported composition date.

notes: Morison repeats the older James catalog's ‘early fifteenth century’ dating, which cannot describe a copy of a work composed around 1456; it is preserved as a historical conflict rather than normalized.

scope.description: Part I excerpts and the surviving Part II as selected by Henry Wharton

date_statements.display: 1909 (first complete printed edition of the surviving text)

scope.description: All text surviving in B.14.45, not the work's lost leaves or ending

assertion.notes: Morison states that a complete text was his first object and characterizes Wharton's 1688 publication as partial.

notes: England is the work's linguistic, ecclesiastical, and intended cultural setting. Kelly reports an argument that Pecock wrote mainly for a London audience, but audience does not establish the exact place of composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's generic eng code describes the bibliographic item without distinguishing Pecock's Middle English from Morison's modern English editorial matter.

scope.description: Biblical, patristic, doctrinal, and scholastic quotations and terms embedded in the Middle English argument

form_statements.description: A prose theological dialogue between a father and son; in Part I chapter 8 the son is temporarily displaced by a Lollard interlocutor and the dialogue becomes more hortatory.

assertion.notes: The work argues against lay and Lollard resistance to clerical authority while also rejecting a simple appeal to ecclesiastical infallibility.

subject_statements.label: The Apostles' Creed and lay religious instruction

summaries.value: A late Middle English theological dialogue in which Pecock seeks a rational way to restore lay dissenters to obedience without grounding that obedience in ecclesiastical infallibility. The prologue frames the work as a response to English lay readers who privilege their own reading of Scripture over clerical teaching. Part I defines faith, argues that evidence and reason strengthen rather than destroy it, and maintains that Christians owe obedience to the church unless its error can be publicly and irrefutably proved. Part II tests the relation between church and Scripture through arguments about oral tradition, apostolic teaching, interpretation, the Apostles' Creed, and whether the clergy can create new articles of faith. The sole manuscript loses two leaves internally and breaks off during Part II chapter 5.

extent.description: The extant structure is one prologue, ten Part I chapter containers, and five Part II chapter containers. Within BODY the XML contains 198 PB, 251 folio MILESTONE, 111 P, and 89 NOTE1 elements, with no L or LG elements. The witness lacks two leaves in quire 2 and all material after the abrupt end at current f.126v, so 15 surviving chapters are not a certain count of the originally composed whole.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: Diagnoses lay disobedience and reliance on private biblical interpretation, rejects clerical infallibility as an effective ground of persuasion, proposes obedience to fallible teachers where error has not been proved, and presents vernacular apologetic writing as a means of engaging dissenters.

content_parts.label: Part I
content_parts.summary: Ten chapters define faith and its relation to reason and evidence, reject accounts that make faith an unsupported act of will or private illumination, and argue for obedience to the church despite its possible fallibility. Chapter 8 directly confronts a Lollard interlocutor; the section closes by insisting that private dissent must prove ecclesiastical error in public disputation.
notes: The gap in quire 2 removes the end of chapter 1 and beginning of chapter 2. The XML's legacy foliation skips 12 and 13; the current Trinity sequence remains 126 physical folios.

content_parts.label: Part II
content_parts.summary: Five surviving chapters examine whether church or Scripture is the chief ground of faith. Pecock treats oral tradition, apostolic preaching and writing, the compilation of Genesis, scriptural interpretation, apostolic succession, the Apostles' Creed, and the church's inability to create major new articles of faith. The manuscript breaks off during chapter 5.
notes: The explicit is absent because the sole witness ends imperfectly after ‘and also thei overleepen this article.’

related_works.title: The Represser of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy
notes: The prologue says The Book of Faith complements arguments made in the first part of the Represser.

related_works.title: The Just Apprising of Holy Scripture
notes: The prologue directly refers readers to the first two parts of this work.

related_works.title: The Follower to the Donet
notes: Part I opens by asking for further teaching about faith beyond that given in the first part of the Follower to the Donet.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, ajh1649.xml.
evidence.summary: Identifies U-M item AJH1649.0001.001, Morison's 1909 source edition, the OCR and page-image workflow, and the surviving prologue and two parts with printed-page and legacy-folio milestones.

Counts three BODY DIV1 units, fifteen DIV2 chapter containers, 198 PB, 251 folio MILESTONE, 111 P, and 89 NOTE1 elements, with no L or LG. The part counts are prologue 5 P/13 PB/15 milestones, Part I 56 P/113 PB/141 milestones/10 chapters, and Part II 50 P/72 PB/95 milestones/5 chapters.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, AJH1649.0001.001.
evidence.locator: item identity, bibliographic source, digital text, linked page images, and rights presentation
evidence.summary: Provides the public discovery route for the U-M item represented by the repository XML. Automated requests met a Cloudflare challenge on 2026-07-15; that access-control response is not evidence that the public item has been withdrawn.

evidence.citation: Reginald Pecock, Reginald Pecock's Book of Faith: A Fifteenth Century Theological Tractate, ed. J. L. Morison (Glasgow: James MacLehose and Sons, 1909).
evidence.locator: title page; preface pp. 9–17; summary pp. 95–105; text pp. 109–305; Wharton reading at p. 236
evidence.summary: Complete 324-page scan supporting the edition's identity, direct Trinity-manuscript basis, complete-surviving-text scope, transcription and punctuation method, chapter summaries, printed and legacy-folio ranges, manuscript losses, and limited comparison with Wharton.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, online manuscript catalog, B.14.45, James no. 329.
evidence.locator: identity, physical description, provenance, collation, contents, and IIIF link
evidence.summary: Identifies the sole known fifteenth-century parchment witness, 126-folio current extent, 17 × 12 cm dimensions, 22 lines per page, Middle English language, Whitgift provenance, missing leaves in quire 2, lost ending, exact three-part structure, and current f.1–126b content boundaries.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge IIIF Presentation manifest for B.14.45.
evidence.locator: 260 canvases; front-cover through back-cover; f001r canvas 5; f126v canvas 256
evidence.summary: Defines the complete digitized codex, exact image services and labels, work mapping to current fols. 1r–126v/canvases 5–256, Trinity attribution, and CC BY-NC 4.0 item license.

evidence.citation: Reginald Pecock, A Treatise Proving Scripture to Be the Rule of Faith, ed. Henry Wharton (London: James Adamson, 1688), EEBO-TCP A53931 XML.
evidence.locator: sourceDesc; Wharton preface pp. xxxix–xl; transcribed Pecock text pp. i–xli
evidence.summary: Wharton says he transcribed the Trinity manuscript, omitted the prologue and most of Part I, printed selected Part I fragments, and published Part II complete as far as the imperfect manuscript allowed. The cached XML is a later EEBO-TCP encoding, not the 1688 print object itself.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive, EEBO-TCP Phase I item A53931.
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1688 source, 2003-05 digital issue, 44 source TIFF images, public XML download, Historical Texts image route, and CC0 dedication of the keyboarded text and markup.

evidence.citation: Henry Ansgar Kelly, ‘Judicial Processes for and against Bishop Reginald Pecock: New Perspectives on the Mechanisms of His Downfall,’ Viator 52.1 (2021): 359–430, doi:10.1484/J.VIATOR.5.130891.
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed modern scholarship dating The Book of Faith c. 1456–57, identifying Morison's edition and Smith's 2020 translation, and situating the work within the events surrounding Pecock's 1457 proceedings.

open_questions.question: Can the composition date and place be established more narrowly than c. 1456–1457 in England?
next_steps: Consult current Pecock chronology and manuscript studies that distinguish intended audience, residence, drafting, revision, and circulation.
Do not convert an argued London audience into a place-of-composition claim without direct evidence.

open_questions.question: How does Morison's legacy 1a–129b foliation map surface by surface to Trinity's current f.1r–126v image sequence?
next_steps: Retain both systems in parallel and collate the 251 XML milestones against all 252 current folio images.

Test whether the two physically missing leaves in quire 2 explain two of the three folio numbers between legacy 129b and current 126v, and resolve the one further discrepancy before publishing a crosswalk.
notes: The XML has no milestones 12a–13b and ends at 129b; the current institutional inventory has 126 folios. These are competing locator systems for one witness, not evidence of multiple manuscripts.

open_questions.question: How many chapters and how much text were lost after the abrupt end of Part II chapter 5?
next_steps: Compare internal cross-references, Wharton's excerpts, Pecock's related works, and modern scholarship without treating Morison's estimate of at least two lost chapters as a complete original count.

open_questions.question: Can U-M supply an exact downloadable bundle and production log for the 316 TIFF page images and 2006 uncorrected OCR?
next_steps: Ask U-M for the item-level image bundle, OCR production record, and clarification of whether the current No Copyright–United States statement covers both OCR/XML and linked TIFFs.
Preserve the XML's generic 2006 distribution warning alongside the newer collection-level rights statement until clarified.

open_questions.question: Will Trinity authorize redistribution of the B.14.45 photographs in a repository that supports commercial and noncommercial print-on-demand publication?
next_steps: Request written publication and repository-reuse permission from wren.library@trin.cam.ac.uk, explicitly describing the Git repository and commercial/POD context.
Keep the complete IIIF cache private and gitignored unless the CC BY-NC terms and any additional Trinity permission clearly cover the proposed redistribution.

notes: The current Trinity catalog's 126-folio inventory supersedes Morison's 127-folio physical count for present-day access, while Morison's 1a–129b milestones remain necessary locators for the 1909 edition and U-M encoding.

Wharton 1688 belongs to historical transmission: it directly transcribes selected material from B.14.45. It is not Morison's continuous base, though Morison reports at least one selected Wharton reading in his apparatus.

The complete Trinity digital object covers all 260 provider canvases, including covers and flyleaves; the target work occupies the internally incomplete manuscript text on current fols. 1r–126v, canvases 5–256.
